perturbation.original_text: THIS AGREEMENT SHALL BE GOVERNED IN ALL RESPECTS, INCLUDING VALIDITY, INTERPRETATION AND EFFECT, BY THE LAWS OF THE STATE OF DELAWARE WITHOUT GIVING EFFECT TO THE CHOICE OF LAW PRINCIPLES OF SUCH STATE.
perturbation.changed_text: THIS AGREEMENT SHALL BE GOVERNED IN ALL RESPECTS, INCLUDING VALIDITY, INTERPRETATION AND EFFECT, BY THE LAWS OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA WITHOUT GIVING EFFECT TO THE CHOICE OF LAW PRINCIPLES OF SUCH STATE.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Delaware to California impacts shareholder rights and corporate governance, especially concerning fiduciary duties. Delaware law offers more management protections. This change introduces a legal contradiction if the company's operations disproportionately affect areas governed by other laws that would be applicable under California's choice-of-law rules but are being avoided by the change in law. If the company is headquartered in California, Delaware law is not applicable in most instances.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Corporations Code § 2115 applies certain California corporate governance laws to foreign corporations (i.e., those not incorporated in California) if they meet specific criteria related to their business and shareholder base within California. By selecting California law in the agreement, without appropriately considering whether Section 2115 might apply, it introduces uncertainty and potential contradiction with Delaware law, which would otherwise govern internal corporate affairs.

CORPORATIONS [100 - 14707] ( Title 1 enacted by Stats. 1947, Ch. 1038. ) DIVISION 1. GENERAL CORPORATION LAW [100 - 2319] ( Division 1 repealed and added by Stats. 1975, Ch. 682. ) CHAPTER 21. Foreign Corporations [2100 - 2117.1] ( Chapter 21 added by Stats. 1975, Ch. 682. ) 2115. (a) A foreign corporation (other than a foreign association or foreign nonprofit corporation but including a foreign parent corporation even though it does not itself transact intrastate business) is subject to the requirements of subdivision (b) commencing on the date specified in subdivision (d) and continuing until the date specified in subdivision (e) if: (1) The average of the property factor, the payroll factor, and the sales factor (as defined in Sections 25129, 25132, and 25134 of the Revenue and Taxation Code) with respect to it is more than 50 percent during its latest full income year and (2) more than one-half of its outstanding voting securities are held of record by persons having addresses in this state appearing on the books of the corporation on the record date for the latest meeting of shareholders held during its latest full income year or, if no meeting was held during that year, on the last day of the latest full income year. The prope
perturbation.justification: The change from Delaware to California law potentially contradicts legal obligations because California Corporations Code § 2115 could apply to the foreign corporation if it meets certain business and shareholder criteria within California, as stated in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 details these criteria, specifying that if the corporation's property, payroll, and sales factors in California exceed 50%, and over half its voting securities are held by California residents, it becomes subject to California corporate governance laws. This contradicts the original intent of using Delaware law to govern internal corporate affairs, particularly if the corporation meets the conditions outlined in § 2115.

perturbation.original_text: FURTHERMORE, EACH OF THE PARTIES HERETO (A) IRREVOCABLY WAIVES THE RIGHT TO TRIAL BY JURY AND (B) AGREES TO WAIVE ANY BONDING REQUIREMENT UNDER ANY APPLICABLE LAW, IN THE CASE ANY OTHER PARTY SEEKS TO ENFORCE THE TERMS BY WAY OF EQUITABLE RELIEF.
perturbation.changed_text: FURTHERMORE, EACH OF THE PARTIES HERETO (A) IRREVOCABLY WAIVES THE RIGHT TO TRIAL BY JURY.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the waiver of bonding requirements for equitable relief could violate legal requirements for providing security in certain litigation situations. This creates a legal loophole.
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 65(c) generally requires a party seeking a preliminary injunction or temporary restraining order to give security (a bond) to cover damages if the injunction is later found to be wrongfully issued. Removing the explicit waiver of this bonding requirement may make it more difficult or costly to obtain injunctive relief, contrary to the intentions of the agreement and potentially violating the spirit of ensuring swift equitable remedies.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders (a) Preliminary Injunction. (1) Notice. The court may issue a preliminary injunction only on notice to the adverse party. (2) Consolidating the Hearing with the Trial on the Merits. Before or after beginning the hearing on a motion for a preliminary injunction, the court may advance the trial on the merits and consolidate it with the hearing. Even when consolidation is not ordered, evidence that is received on the motion and that would be admissible at trial becomes part of the trial record and need not be repeated at trial. But the court must preserve any party's right to a jury trial. (b) Temporary Restraining Order. (1) Issuing Without Notice. The court may issue a temporary restraining order without written or oral notice to the adverse party or its attorney only if: (A) specific facts in an affidavit or a verified complaint clearly show that immediate and irreparable injury, loss, or damage will result to the movant before the adverse party can be heard in opposition; and (B) the movant's attorney certifies in writing any efforts made to give notice and the reasons why it should not be required. (2) Contents; Expiration. Every temporary restraining order issued without notice must state the date and hour it was issued; describe the injury and state why it is irreparable; state why the order was issued without notice; and be promptly filed in the clerk's office and entered in the record. The order expires at the time after entry—not to exceed 14 days—that the court sets, unless before that time the court, for good cause, extends it for a like period or the adverse party consents to a longer extension. The reasons for an extension must be entered in the record. (3) Expediting the Preliminary-Injunction Hearing. If the order is issued without notice, the moti
perturbation.justification: The original contract explicitly waives bonding requirements when a party seeks to enforce the terms by way of equitable relief. The changed text removes this waiver. The Law Explanation states that Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 65(c) generally requires a party seeking a preliminary injunction or temporary restraining order to give security (a bond). By removing the waiver, the modified contract creates a situation where a bond *could* be required, potentially conflicting with the original intent of the agreement to streamline equitable remedies. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing Rule 65, further supports this by outlining the procedures for injunctions and restraining orders, implying the potential applicability of bonding requirements. Therefore, by omitting the explicit waiver, the modified text introduces a legal loophole and undermines the original agreement, potentially leading to non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: (b) The Company hereby agrees that it will not make any acquisitions during the Cooperation Period, except for ordinary course acquisitions individually under $10 million (not to exceed $30 million in the aggregate) or acquisitions that a majority of the JANA Nominees have recommended.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) The Company hereby agrees that it will not make any acquisitions during the Cooperation Period, except for ordinary course acquisitions individually under $10 million (not to exceed $30 million in the aggregate).
perturbation.explanation: By removing the exception for acquisitions recommended by a majority of the JANA Nominees, the company's flexibility is unduly restricted, which could conflict with the board's fiduciary duty to act in the best interest of the shareholders under Delaware law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware General Corporation Law Section 141(a) states that the business and affairs of a corporation shall be managed by or under the direction of a board of directors. By removing the board's ability to authorize acquisitions recommended by the JANA nominees, even if they are in the best interest of the company, the agreement infringes upon the board's fundamental management authority and may be considered a breach of fiduciary duty.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 8 > Chapter 1 Authenticated PDF Â§ 141 Â§ 142 Â§ 143 Â§ 144 Â§ 145 Â§ 146 Â§ 147 TITLE 8 Corporations CHAPTER 1. General Corporation Law Subchapter IV. Directors and Officers Â§ 141. Board of directors; powers; number, qualifications, terms and quorum; committees; classes of directors; nonstock corporations; reliance upon books; action without meeting; removal. (a) The business and affairs of every corporation organized under this chapter shall be managed by or under the direction of a board of directors, except as may be otherwise provided in this chapter or in its certificate of incorporation. If any such provision is made in the certificate of incorporation, the powers and duties conferred or imposed upon the board of directors by this chapter shall be exercised or performed to such extent and by such person or persons as shall be provided in the certificate of incorporation. (b) The board of directors of a corporation shall consist of 1 or more members, each of whom shall be a natural person. The number of directors shall be fixed by, or in the manner provided in, the bylaws, unless the certificate of incorporation fixes the number of directors, in which case a change in the number of directors shall be made only by amendment of the certificate. Directors need not be stockholders unless so required by the certificate of incorporation or the bylaws. The certificate of incorporation or bylaws may prescribe other qualifications for directors. Each director shall hold office until such directorâs successor is elected and qualified or until such directorâs earlier resignation or removal. Any director may resign at any time upon notice given in writing or by electronic transmission to the corporation. A resignation is effective when the resignation is delivered unless the resignation specifies a later effective date or an effective date determined upon the happening of an event or events. A resignation which is conditioned upon the director failing to receive a specified vote for reelection as a direc
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the board's ability to authorize acquisitions recommended by the JANA nominees, which infringes upon the board's fundamental management authority. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically section 141(a), states that the business and affairs of every corporation shall be managed by or under the direction of a board of directors. By removing this specific authorization, the company's board is restricted from acting on recommendations, which conflicts with their duty as stated in 141(a).
